stories.title: Black Woman Therapist Defeats DoorDash in Federal Trademark Battle — Without a Lawyer
stories.summary: Dr. Ashley Bryant, a Black therapist from Oklahoma, represented herself against DoorDash's trademark challenge and won permanently. The $70-billion company tried to block her mental health software platform "LeadDash," but the federal trademark board dismissed their case with prejudice in February 2026.
stories.extended_summary: A licensed therapist with a PhD just defeated a $70-billion tech giant in federal court — without hiring a single lawyer. Dr. Ashley Bryant built LeadDash, software that helps therapists manage client leads and grow their practices. DoorDash filed a trademark opposition claiming the name would confuse consumers with their "DASH" family of brands. Bryant represented herself through the entire legal battle. On February 18, 2026, the United States Patent and Trademark Office permanently dismissed DoorDash's case, and her trademark will proceed to full federal registration.
stories.display_reason: hero always uses image mode (summary always visible)

stories.title: Delafield kids honored for saving their grandfather's life
stories.summary: Everett, 8, and Annemarie, 6, saved their grandfather's life after his heart stopped during bedtime story hour. The Delafield siblings called 911, ran outside to redirect first responders to the correct house, and kept their cool while paramedics revived their grandfather Jeff, who later received a pacemaker.
stories.extended_summary: "Everything went blank" mid-bedtime story, and their grandfather Jeff collapsed, his heart stopped. Everett, 8, and Annemarie, 6, were terrified — but they called 911 anyway. When police arrived at the wrong house, the kids sprinted outside in the January cold to wave them down. Their mother was 15 minutes away. Paramedics revived Jeff, who spent four days in the hospital getting a pacemaker. On March 2, Delafield police honored the siblings with letters of commendation and challenge coins. Chief Landon Nyren said the outcome could have been very different without their quick thinking.

stories.title: Hot air balloon rescue 900 feet in the air
stories.summary: Two passengers were trapped nearly 1,000 feet in the air after their hot air balloon snagged on a Texas cell tower. Longview firefighters executed a high-angle rescue using multiple 300-foot ropes to lower the terrified pair safely to the ground as their basket swung in the wind.
stories.extended_summary: The rainbow hot air balloon basket was "swinging pretty good in the breeze" almost 1,000 feet above Texas, with two passengers clinging to the edges for their lives. The balloon had collided with a cell tower Saturday morning, leaving the pair dangling in mid-air. Longview Fire Department launched a high-angle rescue just before 9 a.m., captured on heart-stopping drone footage. First responders used multiple 300-foot ropes to extract the shaken passengers and lower them "rope by rope" back to solid ground. Both survived unharmed, though Lieutenant Steve Winchell noted the basket's violent swinging made the rescue especially dangerous.

stories.title: Black Man Gets Kidney Transplant from Brother After 8 Years on Dialysis
stories.summary: Keith Preston spent eight years on dialysis, sleeping sitting up because fluid buildup made breathing nearly impossible. His older brother Willis overcame his own fears to donate a kidney in February 2026. The ten-hour surgery succeeded, and both brothers are now advocating for organ donation awareness.
stories.extended_summary: "The fluid was choking me out to the point where it felt like death." For eight years, Keith Preston couldn't lie down to sleep. His remaining kidney was functioning at just 14 percent when he asked his older brother Willis for help in 2024. Willis was terrified — what if his other kidney failed? — but testing at University of Mississippi Medical Center confirmed they were a match. On February 17, 2026, the brothers underwent a ten-hour transplant surgery. Both are recovering well. Keith, 55, and Willis, 58, have since partnered with the Mississippi Organ Recovery Agency to advocate for organ donation, especially in the Black community.

stories.title: Black Mom Survives Heart Attack, Credits Her Daughter for Saving Her Life
stories.summary: When B'Lon Calloway stopped breathing and turned blue at home, her 15-year-old daughter Mahogany performed CPR she'd learned from the American Heart Association. The Cleveland teen kept her mother alive until paramedics arrived. Doctors found a 99% artery blockage and inserted a stent, saving B'Lon's life.
stories.extended_summary: B'Lon Calloway took one big breath, then stopped breathing entirely. Her daughter Mahogany, 15, watched her turn blue and cold. "Dear God please let my momma live," Mahogany whispered as she started CPR, a skill she'd learned two years earlier through the American Heart Association's STEM Goes Red program. She stayed on with 911, compressing her mother's chest until paramedics arrived at their Cleveland home in March 2025. At University Hospitals, doctors discovered a 99% blockage in B'Lon's left anterior descending artery and inserted a stent. B'Lon, 42, also survived a heart attack during the episode and believes divine timing brought her home instead of letting her collapse alone in the car.

stories.title: Okizu strong: How NorCal camp helps kids battling cancer through power of community
stories.summary: Six-year-old Mabel Wong went from judo and cherry-picking to a rare blood cancer diagnosis in days. Her family found support through Okizu, a Northern California nonprofit that's helped families facing childhood cancer build community since 1982. The camp brings together kids, parents, and siblings who understand the battle firsthand.
stories.extended_summary: Mabel's eyes were swollen at school spirit day — the first sign something was wrong. The active 6-year-old who loved judo and agility courses had been exhausted for days. Her parents thought she needed more sleep. Then came the fever. Five days and a battery of tests later, doctors diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia, a rare blood cancer. "I really felt like I floated out of my own body," her father Tim recalled. Okizu, a NorCal nonprofit founded in 1982, stepped in to help. The organization serves around 15,000 children and families annually through camp programs that connect kids battling cancer with others who truly understand — creating what they call "Okizu strong."

stories.title: From addiction to redemption: Virginia missionary’s recovery ministry leads to new church
stories.summary: Fred and Casey Weymouth went from living in a car and chasing their next drug fix to running The Fix, a residential recovery program on 30 acres in rural Virginia. The ministry houses 30 people at a time, combining addiction recovery with Christian discipleship. It started with delivering biscuits and the gospel to people still on the streets.
stories.extended_summary: They were living in the backseat of Casey's car, always looking for the next fix. Fred and Casey Weymouth tried every 12-step program but nothing worked until they found faith. Now they run The Fix, a residential recovery and discipleship program housed on 30 forested acres in Tappahannock, Virginia — 46 miles from civilization. The name is a deliberate play on words: Jesus as the ultimate fix. It began simply, with the couple taking breakfast biscuits to people still struggling on the streets of Richmond. Today, 30 people live in their program at a time, removed from the chaos of addiction and homelessness, learning a new way to live through faith and community.

stories.title: At Camp No Limits, disabilities hold no one back from a ride down Saddleback
stories.summary: Camp No Limits brought families to Maine's Saddleback Ski Area in February for kids with limb differences to learn skiing and snowboarding. Children paired one-on-one with adult mentors who have similar conditions, using adaptive equipment and specialized instruction. For many, it was their first time experiencing winter sports in an environment built for their needs.
stories.extended_summary: David Harrell was born missing his right hand. Twelve years ago, he started volunteering with Camp No Limits because a younger version of himself would have wanted exactly this: a place where limb difference isn't a barrier to flying down a mountain. On February 27, around ten families gathered at Saddleback Ski Area in Rangeley, Maine. Some kids skied solo, others were tethered to adults, and some used adaptive monoskis. Each child was paired one-on-one with a mentor who shares their experience. "I want everybody to be able to experience any type of recreation," said operations director Kelsey Moody. "They have to be able to love our backyard" — especially in a state built for the outdoors.

stories.title: The West Philly club teaching boys to be gentlemen
stories.summary: Dwayne Eric Frazier, a West Philadelphia teacher and retired firefighter, created the Distinguished Young Gentlemen club to teach boys confidence, respect, and self-presentation. Every other week, over two dozen students suit up, practice firm handshakes, and carry themselves differently. For most, it's the first time they've ever worn a suit.
stories.extended_summary: "The first time I ever wore a suit was when I joined the Distinguished Young Gentlemen. It feels good. I think I look handsome in it." More than two dozen boys walk into Lewis C. Cassidy Academics Plus School every other week wearing full suits, ties, and polished shoes. They shake hands with firm grips and hold eye contact. At the center is Dwayne Eric Frazier — teacher, retired firefighter, ordained reverend — who built the Distinguished Young Gentlemen club (DYG) from an after-school idea into a registered nonprofit. Growing up in North Philly, Frazier's father taught him how to tie a tie and look people in the eye. Those lessons stuck through military service and 26 years with the Philadelphia Fire Department. Now he's passing them forward.

stories.title: Singing mailman serenades woman who lost her husband, and people rally to get him a life-changing gift
stories.summary: Lavonte Harvey, a 23-year-old mailman, sang daily on his delivery route — becoming a lifeline for a widow who'd lost her husband of 50 years. When a birthday serenade video went viral with 14 million views, strangers raised enough money to buy him a truck, cutting his brutal 2.5-hour commute to 30 minutes.
stories.extended_summary: Lavonte Harvey sang every day on his mail route, and for one grandmother who'd just lost her husband of fifty years, that voice was "her daily dose of life." Her granddaughter Whitney Cumbo, a financial literacy teacher, asked Harvey to sing for her grandmother's birthday. The video exploded — 14 million views. Then Cumbo learned Harvey commuted two-and-a-half hours each way to work. She started a GoFundMe. Strangers donated enough to buy the 23-year-old mailman a truck, slashing his commute to half an hour. "I'm able to walk my dog now in the morning," Harvey said. "I'm able to have more of a personal life. It's not all just work-life based." Joy shared became joy multiplied.

stories.title: Foley 11-Year-Old Turns Heartbreak Into Hope
stories.summary: After her 4-year-old brother Gardner was diagnosed with leukemia, 11-year-old Mary Grace Glover transformed grief into action. She designed encouraging T-shirts for him during treatment, then committed herself to learning everything she can about finding a cure. Her determination turned fear into strength as she supports her best friend through his toughest battle.
stories.extended_summary: Mary Grace Glover was on the phone with Gardner two weeks before his diagnosis, listening to him sing and laugh. Now she watches IV poles and medical alarms frame his hospital bed instead. The 11-year-old refused to let that diagnosis be the end of his joy. She started by making bright T-shirts covered in messages of hope, wrapping her little brother in what she calls "armor made of love." But the shirts were just the beginning — Mary Grace made a promise in that quiet moment after the doctors left: she's going to help find a cure. In their single-parent home in Foley, Alabama, she's turned her fear into something fierce and purposeful, determined that Gardner will keep dancing and singing "forever."

stories.title: Dozens of Strangers Form Parade for Man with Cancer, Driving Classic Cars Passed his Home for One Last Surprise (WATCH)
stories.summary: When 80-year-old Max Archuleta's terminal cancer left him too weak to attend car shows, his granddaughter posted a request online expecting a few vintage cars. Instead, 50 classic car owners lined up to parade past his Lakewood home, honking and waving. One driver even taped Max's photo to his 1951 Ford Shoebox as tribute.
stories.extended_summary: Max Archuleta had spent a lifetime at car shows, running his hands along chrome bumpers and round mirrors of vintage beauties. But terminal cancer stole his energy, leaving him homebound and cut off from what he loved most. His granddaughter Annaliesse posted a simple request online, hoping maybe a handful of cars would drive by. Fifty showed up. They formed a single-file parade through his Lakewood neighborhood — every color, some flying American flags, every driver honking and waving at the man in the doorway. Butch Souza taped Max's photo to his 1951 Ford Shoebox because "that's something you do for your community." Max couldn't believe it was all for him.

stories.title: Sharing life on the streets to sharing a stage: Mother and son defeat the odds and return to college
stories.summary: Herica Paniagua survived her teenage son's suicide, 15 years of addiction, and over a decade without stable housing. Now at 52, she's walking across the stage at Long Beach City College this spring to graduate alongside her son Edrick — the same son who found his brother's body at age seven and never stopped believing in his mother.
stories.extended_summary: Edrick Salgado was seven years old when he found his 17-year-old brother's body. His mother Herica Paniagua was already a certified drug and alcohol counselor, a single parent holding everything together through work and school. That July day in 2010 ripped it all apart. Addiction pulled her under for 15 years. She lost stable housing. She lost more than a decade to the streets. But Edrick, now grown, never stopped believing she could come back. This spring, at 52, Herica will walk across the A-quad at Long Beach City College — a walk she once thought she'd never make again — and graduate alongside the son who waited for her. Every step across that quad is a reminder of how close she came to losing everything, and how far love carried them both.

stories.title: A Fire Inside: Merced Firefighter Rhett Avant Returns to Duty After Life-Changing Crash
stories.summary: After a motorcycle crash mangled his foot on Good Friday 2025, Merced firefighter Rhett Avant tourniqueted his own leg, called 911, and directed bystanders to his medical kit before the ambulance arrived. Ten months later, he walked back into the fire bay as a full-duty firefighter, putting his gear on the rig for the first time since the accident.
stories.extended_summary: Rhett Avant was lying in the middle of the road on April 18, 2025, his foot mangled after his motorcycle caught the rear dual of a semi-trailer. He could have panicked. Instead, he butt-scooted to the shoulder so he wouldn't get hit again, directed bystanders to his saddle bag medical kit, tourniqueted his own leg, took over the 911 call, gave dispatchers his exact location, then phoned his fire chief to report what happened — all before the ambulance arrived. Ten months of recovery followed. On February 18, 2026, Rhett walked back into the Merced fire station bay, took a breath, and looked at the rig. "We're doing this," he told himself. "I'm actually putting my gear on the rig." He had earned every heartbeat of that moment.

stories.title: Feeding the Blizzard
stories.summary: Sandy Bessingpas has opened her rural Holmes City home to nearly 60 hockey players over 15 years, after answering an ad seeking host families for the Alexandria Blizzard team. Now a widow living alone, she houses three players from across the country because "I love to cook and bake, but not for just one."
stories.extended_summary: Fifteen years ago, Sandy Bessingpas saw an ad in the paper looking for host families for the Blizzard Hockey team and thought, "I've got empty bedrooms." She admits she was naive about what she was getting into. Nearly 60 players later — from Colorado, Wisconsin, and across the country — Sandy's rural Holmes City home has become a second family for young athletes far from their own. After her husband Glenn died in 2006 and her two sons grew up and moved away, she could have stayed quiet in her home with her quilts and garden. Instead, she chose noise and life. "I always like to have people around," she said. "I love to cook and bake, but not for just one." This year, three Blizzard players call her home theirs, surrounded by her cooking and the warmth of someone who decided loneliness wasn't the only option.

stories.title: UPSIDE: Pastors now ‘twin brothers’ after kidney donation
stories.summary: When Rev. Raymund Reyes faced stage 3 chronic kidney disease and years of dialysis, his friend Rev. Francis Mark Garbo stepped forward with an extraordinary gift: his kidney. The two Filipino American priests, connected by faith and vocation for nearly three decades, are now "twin brothers" in a way neither expected.
stories.extended_summary: Rev. Raymund Reyes was facing a future of dialysis three times a week, his blood filtered through machines as his kidneys failed. Diagnosed with stage 3 chronic kidney disease in 2019, the same year he became pastor of St. Augustine Catholic Church in South San Francisco, he tried peritoneal dialysis first — a strict daily protocol he administered himself. When that wasn't enough, hemodialysis followed. Then Rev. Francis Mark Garbo, his friend of nearly 30 years and fellow priest, did something unheard of in the Archdiocese of San Francisco: he donated his kidney. Not a relative. Not a parishioner. A brother in faith. Twenty days into 2026, the surgery gave Reyes "the gift of a second life." Now Reyes calls Garbo his hero, his saint, his twin brother — bound not just by vocation but by blood and sacrifice.

stories.title: They came back to plant what the flood took away
stories.summary: Nine months after catastrophic July 4th flooding killed 117 people and reshaped the Guadalupe River, volunteers and biologists planted over 2,000 native tree cuttings along its banks. Led by Texas Parks and Wildlife biologist Gaby Tamez, the effort marks the beginning of long-term restoration for the scarred riverbanks.
stories.extended_summary: The Guadalupe River was torn apart on July 4, 2025. Floodwaters claimed 117 lives in Kerrville and left the banks scarred with debris and broken trees. Recovery came slowly — months of clearing trash, hauling away what the water destroyed. Then Texas Parks and Wildlife biologist Gaby Tamez arrived with a trailer full of 2,000 tree cuttings and a tight timeline. "We don't have a whole lot of time today to plant over 2,000 trees," she told volunteers gathered along the riverbanks. These weren't typical trees — they had no roots yet, just cuttings that would need time and care to stabilize the banks and restore what the flood ripped away. It's a fraction of what was lost, but it's a start. And along the Guadalupe, the work of dozens of hands marks a turning point from destruction toward something growing again.

stories.title: How Deciding to Donate a Kidney Saved My Own Life
stories.summary: When the author filled out a living kidney donation application hoping to help someone else, routine medical screening revealed a life-threatening condition in her own body. The decision to donate didn't just save a stranger — it saved her own life by catching a problem that might have gone undetected until too late.
stories.extended_summary: She'd seen the signs everywhere — literally. A sandwich board on the side of the road: "NEED KIDNEY 4 WIFE." Posters near her daughter's high school asking people to register as donors. A documentary about altruistic kidney donation that played on a seatback screen during a flight. Every time the idea faded, something reminded her that over 90,000 people were waiting, many on dialysis, many facing chronic illness and financial ruin. So she filled out the National Kidney Registry application, hoping to improve someone else's life. She never expected the routine medical screening to reveal a life-threatening condition in her own body — one that might have gone undetected until it was too late. The decision to donate a kidney didn't just save a stranger. It saved her.

stories.title: Good Samaritans Sprang into Action to Save Baby That Fell into Lake Michigan
stories.summary: When fierce wind caught a baby's bassinet at Chicago's Belmont Harbor and hurled it into Lake Michigan, 30-year-old Lio Cundiff jumped in without a plan. He kept the stroller from sinking until American Airlines pilot Luis Kapost arrived, tossed him a Cubs jacket arm to hold, and together they lifted the baby to safety.
stories.extended_summary: Lio Cundiff was on the phone with his aunt at Belmont Harbor when the wind picked up and a woman's scream tore through the air. He knew instantly: the wind had caught a bassinet and sent baby, cradle, and all into Lake Michigan. The mother was frozen in panic. So Cundiff jumped. "I had no idea what the plan was," he admitted. He reached the stroller but couldn't free the baby, so he lifted the top half out of the water to stop it sinking — treading water with 30+ pounds of soaking wet metal and fabric. That's when Luis Kapost, an American Airlines pilot, ran up and tossed one arm of his Chicago Cubs jacket to Cundiff, holding the other end to keep him afloat. Together, with the nanny's help, they lifted the stroller out. First responders took baby and hero to the hospital. Cundiff called work, called his girlfriend, and went about his day — just another Chicagoan who saw someone in trouble and jumped.

stories.title: New Orleans brings back the house call, sending nurses to visit newborns and moms
stories.summary: New Orleans is reviving the house call to improve maternal and infant health outcomes, among the worst in the nation. Through Family Connects, registered nurses visit new parents at home to check on babies, offer hands-on help with breastfeeding and sleep routines, and catch health issues early — all without parents needing to leave the house.
stories.extended_summary: Lisa Bonfield was exhausted, still figuring out the infant car seat, when a registered nurse knocked on her door. Her daughter Adele was only a few weeks old, and Bonfield was drowning in questions she never thought to ask before the baby arrived: breastfeeding, colic, crying, diapering, all the little warning signs something could be wrong. But she didn't have to pack everything up for a clinic trip. The nurse came to her. New Orleans, facing some of the worst maternal and infant health outcomes in the nation, is bringing back the old-fashioned house call through Family Connects. Registered nurses visit families at home up to three times after birth, offering hands-on help and catching problems early. Louisiana's numbers are grim, but the city is betting that returning to this personal, practical approach — meeting families where they are, literally — can turn things around.

stories.title: Las Vegas first responders conduct seven-hour rescue of injured climber stranded 600 feet up rock face
stories.summary: Las Vegas first responders spent seven hours scaling a 600-foot rock face to rescue an injured climber. The team worked through dangerous terrain to bring the stranded climber safely back to ground level.
stories.extended_summary: A climber dangled 600 feet up a sheer rock face, injured and unable to move. For seven hours, Las Vegas first responders scaled the wall, navigating loose rock and exposure, to reach them. The technical rescue required specialized equipment and coordination between multiple teams. Against gravity and time, they secured the climber and brought them down safely—a reminder that some people run toward danger so others can make it home.

stories.title: From potholes to progress: neighbors fund repairs on Fuller Road
stories.summary: Residents of Three Points, Arizona raised over $5,000 to repair their pothole-riddled main road after the county said it couldn't maintain it. Twenty-six neighbors pitched in, funding contractor work that filled holes with 100 tons of material, with plans to continue repairs.
stories.extended_summary: Fuller Road had become nearly undrivable—deep holes forced drivers to crawl and swerve just to get through. Because the road is a private easement, Pima County couldn't touch it. So Larry Worden and Kevin Kassner knocked on doors, posted signs, and asked neighbors to chip in. Twenty-six people donated over $5,000. A local contractor hauled in 100 tons of material and filled the worst holes. The road isn't perfect yet, but it's passable—and it got that way because neighbors decided not to wait for someone else to fix it.
stories.display_reason: medium image not worth the space

stories.title: Albuquerque teens bridge generations with GlamourGals at senior center
stories.summary: Two Albuquerque sisters started a GlamourGals chapter after watching their grandmother cling to them during a goodbye, desperate for connection. Now they visit senior centers monthly with manicures, crafts, and conversation—combating isolation one painted nail at a time.
stories.extended_summary: Their grandmother grabbed their arms as they tried to leave, pleading with them to stay. Dana and Jenna Poon realized she wasn't the only one. They founded the first New Mexico chapter of GlamourGals, a national group fighting senior isolation through social events. Once a month, they bring nail polish, tote bags, and paint to senior centers—but mostly, they bring their time. Florence Miller says she loves the manicures, but it's the company that matters. The seniors ask the teens questions. The teens listen. And for a few hours, no one is alone.

stories.title: Wakulla Senior Center crochet class donates handmade blankets and hats to newborns in the county
stories.summary: A crochet class at Wakulla Senior Center handmakes blankets and hats for every newborn in the county. The Wakulla Pregnancy Center gives each new mother a blanket with her positive test—a tangible first gift that says "this is real, you're having a baby."
stories.extended_summary: One woman had been trying to get pregnant for years. When she finally got a positive test at the Wakulla Pregnancy Center, they handed her a handmade baby blanket—soft, real, proof that this was happening. She started crying. The blankets come from a crochet class at the local senior center, where women spend their afternoons making gifts for babies they'll never meet. Eileen Bramblett, who runs the class, says it's about giving young mothers something comforting, something they can keep forever. The seniors hook yarn into rows. The rows become blankets. The blankets become the first thing a new life touches.
stories.display_reason: medium image but highly visual story

stories.title: Benito, a raccoon rescued by South Philly neighbors during cold spell, is on the mend at a busy wildlife center
stories.summary: South Philly neighbors spent 10 days trying to capture Benito, a mange-covered, frostbitten raccoon barely surviving the city's coldest stretch in decades. They finally lured him into a carrier, and now he's regrowing fur and putting on weight at a Lancaster County wildlife center.
stories.extended_summary: For 10 days, Benito wandered South Philly with no fur, open wounds, and frostbite covering his body. He was emaciated and reeked, but neighbors on South Cleveland Street refused to give up. They set out a carrier with food and waited. Finally, he went in. At Raven Ridge Wildlife Center, the thick crust covering his skin has fallen off. His fur is growing back. He's gained weight. "I don't even know how this animal kept warm," said founder Tracie Young. The answer: he barely did. But the community wouldn't let him die trying.

stories.title: United Airlines employees donate 15,000 Pokemon Cards to boy who lost collection in Denver
stories.summary: When 7-year-old Reid lost his Pokémon card collection at Denver Airport, United Airlines employees from around the world raided their personal stashes and sent him 15,000 cards—90 pounds worth. Reid hosted a trading party at school and gave each classmate 60 cards.
stories.extended_summary: Reid's Pokémon binder vanished at Denver International Airport. His dad posted about it online. United's social team saw it and asked how they could help. What happened next: employees from Hawaii to Ireland dug through their personal collections and mailed cards to Denver. The haul weighed 90 pounds—over 15,000 cards. When Reid picked them up at the airport, he didn't hoard them. He threw a trading party at school and sent every classmate home with 60 cards. His family is donating more. One lost binder became a small avalanche of generosity.

stories.title: ‘I want to be someone who people can count on': Woman aims to serve the Karen community in any way she can
stories.summary: Kmwe Win works full-time for Albert Lea schools as a success coach and interpreter, but her real job has no hours. She gets calls at 2 a.m. from Karen community members who need help with appointments, paperwork, or something that broke—and she always shows up.
stories.extended_summary: It's 2 a.m. and Kmwe Win's phone rings. Someone in Albert Lea's Karen community needs help—maybe it's interpreting, maybe it's filling out forms, maybe something broke and they don't know who else to call. She gets up and goes. Win moved to Albert Lea in 2012 and has spent every year since making herself available. Mondays through Fridays she works for the school district. Weekends are for church, where she leads youth programs. But the rest of the time? "Whenever they need me," she says. "I want to be someone who people can count on, even if I can only give a little."

stories.title: Lafayette Soccer Coach Exemplifies What True Sportsmanship Is
stories.summary: When a 6-year-old soccer team in Lafayette was getting crushed, the winning coach stopped the match near the end, lined up the losing team, and had each girl kick the ball into an open goal while both teams and all the parents cheered wildly for every score.
stories.extended_summary: The little girls on the losing team looked defeated—shoulders slumped, faces down. They were younger, less experienced, and getting destroyed. As the match wound down, Coach Underwood stopped play. He called his own team behind the goal and lined up every kid from the other side. One by one, each girl kicked the ball into the net. And one by one, the winning team erupted in cheers. So did the parents. Every single goal got celebrated like a championship. The kids who felt crushed moments before walked off the field grinning. That's what youth sports is supposed to look like.

stories.title: The Divorce Doula: Chantay Sanders helps women turn endings into launchpads
stories.summary: Chantay Sanders calls herself a "Divorce Doula"—she coaches women through what she calls "the labor of letting go." With an MBA from NYU and dual certifications in life and business coaching, she helps women navigate grief, identity crises, and faith struggles during divorce.
stories.extended_summary: Chantay Sanders spent years as a product marketer, project manager, and entrepreneur. But 15 years ago, she walked a close friend through a devastating divorce—and realized she was already doing the work she was meant for. Now she's a Certified Divorce Coach, or as she calls it, a "Divorce Doula." The name is deliberate: just as a birth doula supports women through labor, Sanders supports them through the labor of letting go. She helps women process grief, rebuild identity, and—for those who are believers—wrestle with faith when prayers don't get answered the way they hoped. With an MBA from NYU and decades of leadership experience, she brings both heart and strategy to one of life's hardest transitions.

stories.title: Students, seniors compete and connect in Olympics
stories.summary: Marblehead High juniors organized a full-day Olympics at the Community Center where students and seniors competed in chair volleyball, cornhole, and curling. The Students and Seniors Club usually only meets during lunch, but the teens wanted more time to connect across generations.
stories.extended_summary: Fifteen high schoolers walked down to the Marblehead Community Center at 11 a.m. and spent the next three hours playing chair volleyball, cornhole, and curling with senior citizens. Three juniors—Sadie Halpern, Maren Rowe, and Sydney Berman—spent three months planning the event, coordinating schedules and building rosters. Their Students and Seniors Club usually only meets once a month during a one-hour free period, but they wanted more time. "There's just so much more that you can do in three hours than one," Rowe said. The gym filled with bouncing volleyballs, sliding curling stones, and laughter from both ends of the age spectrum.

stories.title: 24-YO Is on a Mission to Rescue Strays in Delhi: Your Help Can Give Them a Fighting Chance
stories.summary: Naman Sharma started rescuing animals at age 12 after trying to save an injured pigeon. Now 24, he runs a Delhi shelter housing over 200 rescued animals and responding to 20 emergency calls daily. The shelter needs funding for medical equipment to continue saving strays across the city.
stories.extended_summary: A pigeon died in his hands when he was in Class 6, but Naman Sharma didn't give up — he learned everything he could about rescue. Today at 24, the chemistry graduate operates Aashiyana by Naman & Welfare Trust, a registered shelter in Delhi that houses over 200 rescued animals including dogs, monkeys, donkeys, and cats. His team fields up to 20 emergency calls every single day. The shelter urgently needs a veterinary CBC machine to speed up critical blood work and cut medical costs that are piling up fast.

stories.title: Providing an option for home births: Midwife opens practice, providing care in a ‘maternity care desert’
stories.summary: Nikki Meyer opened Deep Wells Midwifery in Wells after giving birth to four of her five children at home. As a certified professional midwife, she's bringing prenatal and home birth services to a rural "maternity care desert" where hospitals have scaled back labor and delivery options. Meyer provides the same care schedule as an OBGYN but with a more personalized approach.
stories.extended_summary: When rural hospitals pulled back their labor and delivery services, expectant mothers in Wells were left wondering where they'd welcome their babies. Nikki Meyer, a mother of five who had four home births herself, saw the gap and filled it. In early 2024, she opened Deep Wells Midwifery as a certified professional midwife licensed in Minnesota and Iowa. She provides full prenatal care on the same schedule as an OBGYN — routine exams, lab work, ultrasound orders — but in what's known as a "maternity care desert." For mothers who want personalized care and the option of home birth, Meyer is bringing that choice back to a community that desperately needed it.

stories.title: With the traditional lion dance, this family shares Chinese culture across Maine
stories.summary: Steve Wong has been performing the traditional Chinese lion dance at Maine schools and events for over 30 years, starting when a Bangor school called looking for someone to teach students about Lunar New Year. Now 76, he's turned it into a family tradition — his son and grandchildren join him at dozens of performances annually, sharing Chinese culture through the dance's fluttering eyes and nimble steps.
stories.extended_summary: When a Bangor-area school called Sing's Polynesian Restaurant in the early 1990s asking for someone to demonstrate Chinese culture for Lunar New Year, Steve Wong borrowed his aunt's lion costume and said yes. Students were mesmerized by the costume's fluttering eyes, brightly painted mouth, and shimmering fur-trimmed body. Three decades later, Wong is still dancing — now 76 years old and turning it into a family affair. His son Rodger, 41, and two grandchildren join him at schools, cultural events, weddings, and restaurant openings across Maine. Inside the Westbrook Performing Arts Center this past Sunday, Rodger donned the papier-mâché head while pulling the red strings that make the lion's pink eyes flutter, bringing the ancient tradition to life for a new generation.

stories.title: Treacherous 43-Day Mountain Search Finally Reunites Dog With Owner After it Bolted During Road Trip: ‘It was amazing’
stories.summary: Steven Maa's dog Rocky bolted into Colorado's Rocky Mountains during a cross-country move, disappearing into a blizzard at 10,000 feet. After 43 days of searching with trail cameras and a nonprofit rescue team, Rocky was finally found and reunited with Steven — who had to leave for his California job before the happy ending. Summit Lost Pet Rescue has recovered over 200 pets in the last year.
stories.extended_summary: Steven Maa left Rocky with a pet sitter in Montezuma, Colorado while he went skiing during a cross-country move from New Jersey to California. But the mottled brown-and-black dog bolted into the mountains just as a blizzard rolled in. At nearly 10,000 feet elevation and temperatures plunging below zero, Steven feared Rocky wouldn't survive. Summit Lost Pet Rescue set up trail cameras and scent stations, and Rocky appeared on camera after a few days — but remained elusive. Steven spent multiple 16-hour days searching before having to leave for his new job in California. For 43 treacherous days, the nonprofit kept searching. Finally, they recovered Rocky and reunited him with Steven, adding another success to their near-perfect record of over 200 pet recoveries in the last year alone.

stories.title: Morning Light, Lifeline for Neighbors: Englewood Meals on Wheels at the Heart of the Community
stories.summary: Director Kathleen Macklin runs the Englewood Meals on Wheels headquarters where volunteers transform a simple packing room into a lifeline for the community. Each morning, the team fills cold coolers with fresh fruit, stacks loaves of bread for weekly deliveries, and adds extras like oversized muffins to hot meal trays. The operation ensures no one in Englewood goes hungry.
stories.extended_summary: Morning sun filters through the windows of Englewood Meals on Wheels headquarters, where director Kathleen Macklin greets volunteers with a smile that's as much a part of the building as the foundation itself. This bustling room is where the logistics of the day meet the heart of the mission. Against one wall, heavy-duty cold coolers hum as staff pack them with fresh cups of fruit. Nearby, stacks of bread loaves reach toward the ceiling — every client gets a loaf each week, a small but crucial staple between deliveries. Today's extras are golden, oversized muffins that go alongside hot meals being prepared in the kitchen. For Macklin, this isn't just a distribution center; it's a sanctuary of service where the simple act of packing a bag becomes a lifeline.

stories.title: Years After Traumatic Snowboarding Accident, Survivor Makes Strides With Brain-Focused Workouts
stories.summary: Thomas Ruble suffered a traumatic brain injury snowboarding at 16, and after years of physical therapy, his family hit a plateau. Then he met Carolayn Crane, a neuro-specialist and Pilates instructor in Ship Bottom who specializes in post-TBI recovery. Defying the medical belief that progress stops after two years, Thomas has made dramatic strides — moving beyond his wheelchair after a decade of being fully dependent on it.
stories.extended_summary: Thomas Ruble was 16 when a snowboarding accident on Hunter Mountain left him with a traumatic brain injury and a gnarly scar. A decade of physical and occupational therapy helped, but by 2024, the Ruble family had plateaued. Thomas was fully dependent on his wheelchair, and doctors said wherever he was is basically where he'd stay — medical consensus holds that recovery stops progressing after two years. Then his sister Mary, studying occupational therapy, connected him with Carolayn Crane, neuro-specialist and owner of Pyour Core Pilates Studio in Ship Bottom. The two had an instant connection. Defying the odds and every expectation, Thomas has made more progress in the last two years than the entire previous decade, moving beyond his wheelchair and proving that recovery timelines aren't set in stone.

stories.title: Take a Breath: Sheriff’s chaplain provides more than religion
stories.summary: Jeff Humphrey spent 25 years as a full-time pastor before moving to Whidbey Island in 2012 to regroup. Now he volunteers as Senior Chaplain for Island County Sheriff's Office and North Whidbey Fire and Rescue, responding to emergencies in the middle of the night and supporting crime victims, families, and first responders. The role lit him on fire again, letting him serve his community in a new way.
stories.extended_summary: After 25 years as a full-time pastor giving his congregation everything he had, Jeff Humphrey needed to step back. In 2012, he and his wife Dawn moved from Idaho to Whidbey Island. He started a sign business and fully intended to pastor again someday. Then the chaplain opportunity came along — and it lit him on fire. As Senior Chaplain for the Island County Sheriff's Office and North Whidbey Fire and Rescue, Humphrey responds to calls in the middle of the night, sits quietly with crime victims and their families, and counsels first responders dealing with their own stresses. It's a big job with no regular hours. But for Humphrey, it's the sweet spot where he makes a difference, he's good at it, and he loves doing it — just in a new way.

stories.title: Lubbock’s Alzheimer’s Football League turns powder-puff game into push for research and care
stories.summary: Lubbock's Alzheimer's Football League raises money through a community draft, scrimmage, and women's powder-puff game each year. Co-chair Jessica Stafford lost her grandmother to Alzheimer's after a decade-long battle. This year, the league is aiming to raise $100,000 for research and caregiver support through Garrison Institute on Aging — more than doubling last year's $40,000 goal.
stories.extended_summary: Jessica Stafford's grandmother battled Alzheimer's for more than a decade, a fight that drove Stafford to co-chair Lubbock's Alzheimer's Football League. The cause is deeply personal, but the mission goes beyond grief — the league partners with Garrison Institute on Aging to fund research and connect caregivers with real help. "It is crazy that we're in 2026 and there's still so little known about Alzheimer's," Stafford said. Now in its fifth season, the league hosts a community draft, May scrimmage, and June powder-puff game at Jones AT&T Stadium. This year, they're aiming for $100,000 — more than double last year's goal — and the committee grew from nine to twenty members. Draft day showed the momentum: hundreds gathered for one cause in a fractured world.
stories.display_reason: medium image but highly visual story

stories.title: From Legacy to Lifeline: Serenity Home Aims to Transform the Futures of Birmingham’s Teen Girls
stories.summary: Tomeka Mills is building A.W. Brooks Serenity Home, the first purpose-built group home for displaced teen girls in Birmingham, to honor her late father's dream of helping the community. After seeing countless foster parent signs and learning that only 17% of adopted foster kids are teenage girls, Tomeka knew she had to act. The home will give girls a safe, stable place when their chances of adoption drop dramatically.
stories.extended_summary: During a drive through Birmingham, Tomeka Mills was shocked by the foster parent signs on every corner. Her daughter, a social worker, explained something heartbreaking: once girls reach their teenage years, their chances of being adopted plummet. Only 17% of foster kids adopted between ages 11 and 16 are girls. Tomeka thought of the displaced girls she'd seen in her Ensley community and felt her late father's dream rise within her. Reverend Arthur W. Brooks had dreamed of helping displaced people but passed before seeing it fulfilled. Now Tomeka is bringing it to life: A.W. Brooks Serenity Home will be Birmingham's first purpose-built group home for teen girls. "I can't save the world," she says. "But if I can do something to save some of these girls, I can make a difference."

stories.title: Benefit at Bakersfield School raises $45,000 for local mother with cancer
stories.summary: The town of Bakersfield (population 186) packed over 200 people into their school for a benefit auction and chili supper supporting Natasha Crites, a local resident battling breast cancer and gallbladder complications. Community members spent weeks soliciting donations from local businesses, and the Feb. 7 event raised $45,000. Bakersfield is known for its grassroots, neighbors-helping-neighbors generosity.
stories.extended_summary: Bakersfield's population is just 186, but on February 7, over 200 people crammed into the school for a benefit auction and chili supper. Their neighbor, Natasha Crites, has been battling breast cancer and life-threatening gallbladder complications that have kept her from working since last summer. The community spent weeks organizing — Amy Ingalsbe, Stephanie Guffey, Joann Foster, Valerie Lockett, Trudy Summers, and countless others solicited donations from local businesses and showed up with items in hand. When the bidding cards went up and chili bowls were scraped clean, the night raised $45,000. Organizers say local businesses "never hesitate" to donate to benefits. It's the Bakersfield way: grassroots, neighbors-helping-neighbors generosity that shows up time and time again.
stories.display_reason: medium image but highly visual story

stories.title: 'You can': How Kylie LaBelle answered Detroit Lakes’ call for firefighters
stories.summary: A Mississippi State student working a service day at a fire station asked a firefighter how to become a volunteer. His two-word answer—"You can"—changed her path. Now she's a biologist in Detroit Lakes and a sworn firefighter, joining a department struggling to recruit volunteers.
stories.extended_summary: "I wish I could do that," Kylie LaBelle told a firefighter while scrubbing engines on a college service day in Mississippi. He looked at her and said, "You can." Those two words flipped a switch. The student realized nothing was stopping her—so she did it. Now she works as a biologist for the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service in Detroit Lakes and serves as a volunteer firefighter, officially sworn in this February. She's a critical addition at a time when volunteer fire departments across the country are desperate for recruits.
stories.display_reason: medium image but highly visual story

stories.title: Student volunteers at west side school send 20,000 meals to Haiti
stories.summary: Fifty students at an Indianapolis middle school packed 20,000 meals for kids in Haiti—enough to feed 100 students for a year. The effort is part of their International Baccalaureate requirement to serve as global citizens. Many volunteers came from Haitian families, and 19 languages are spoken at the school.
stories.extended_summary: Students at Northwest Middle School in Indianapolis spent a Thursday morning packing 20,000 meals bound for Haiti. That's enough food to feed 100 kids for an entire year. The work fulfills their International Baccalaureate diploma requirement: creativity, activity, and service. Many of the student volunteers are from Haitian families themselves, and 19 languages are spoken at the school. The meals will be distributed through churches running schools in Haiti, coordinated by Kids Against Hunger and Mission of Hope.

stories.title: A Night to Shine on the red carpet
stories.summary: Jessica Warren got her hair done, slipped into a blue sequined dress, and stepped onto a red carpet in Richmond Hill for Night to Shine—a prom for people with special needs. The Tim Tebow Foundation event is powered by local students and churches, with donated formal wear and volunteers who make sure everyone gets invited, including nursing home residents.
stories.extended_summary: Jessica Warren sat still as a brush moved through her hair, her blue sequined dress waiting nearby. In a few hours, she'd walk a red carpet, greeted by cheers and cameras at Night to Shine, a prom-style event for people with special needs age 14 and up. Weeks before, families attended fittings stocked with 600 dresses and 200 suits—all donated, all free. Local nursing home staff help residents dress and do makeup before walking them across the street to the church. "They never get invited to things," one coordinator said. "This is the only time all year they leave the facility."

stories.title: Unified students have their day
stories.summary: Cloquet High School's unified PE program—which combines general and special education students—earned national banner recognition from the Special Olympics. At a pep rally, unified students played an exhibition basketball game against teachers, and the crowd went wild every time the students scored. They also compete in regional sports and are preparing for Mario Kart races.
stories.extended_summary: The gym erupted every time a unified student scored. At Cloquet High School, a basketball exhibition pitted students from the school's unified PE program—general and special education combined—against teachers and staff. The crowd was firmly on the students' side, and they won 10-2. The program earned national banner recognition from the Special Olympics in November, and last week's pep rally celebrated that honor. The students compete in regional sports, ran a water station at Grandma's Marathon, and are preparing to race each other in Mario Kart as esports expand in schools.

stories.title: 'What a privilege': Artist sketches portraits of nursing home residents
stories.summary: A local artist sat with nursing home residents in Fergus Falls to sketch their portraits for an exhibit called "Reflections of a Generation." Ten residents posed, and their pencil portraits now hang in the facility's atrium. When the exhibit ends, each sketch goes home with the person it depicts.
stories.extended_summary: Sally Christenson, 91, sat in a chair while artist Scott Gunvaldson sketched her face. "I just sat in the chair, and he did the work," she said, laughing as she looked at the portrait capturing nine decades. "I think he created something out of nothing. I can't believe it." Ten residents of Pioneer Care in Fergus Falls posed for portraits as part of "Reflections of a Generation," an exhibit funded by the Lake Region Arts Council. The pencil sketches now hang in the facility's atrium, and when the exhibit ends, each one goes home with its subject.

stories.title: ‘Miracle Matt’ Reunites With Orlando Firefighters Nearly 12 Years After Life-Saving Rescue
stories.summary: Orlando firefighters reunited with Matthew "Miracle Matt" Manzari, a man they saved nearly 12 years ago after he was electrocuted while trimming trees. He suffered severe burns and trauma but survived—rare for that type of injury. Since then, he's had over 80 surgeries and now travels the country as a motivational speaker.
stories.extended_summary: Matthew Manzari struck a power line while trimming trees in Orlando in 2014. He was electrocuted, severely burned, and traumatically injured—but stayed conscious as bystanders called 911. Firefighters from Firehouse 3 arrived within minutes, stabilized him, and transported him to the hospital. Survival from that kind of injury is rare. Manzari has since undergone more than 80 surgeries and made a remarkable recovery. He returned to wake-skating and now travels as a motivational speaker. This week, he reunited with the crew that saved him. His wife was pregnant with their first child the day of the accident; they now have three kids.

stories.title: Miss Vestavia Hills’ Teen fosters hope on and off stage
stories.summary: Lyla Smith won Miss Vestavia Hills' Teen—and her older sister crowned her on stage while her younger sister stood beside her as Rising Star. A survivor of childhood abuse, Lyla now uses pageants to raise money and support for foster children through her platform, Fostering Hope. She competes for Miss Alabama's Teen in March.
stories.extended_summary: Lyla Smith stood on stage as her older sister, Miss Vestavia Hills 2025, placed the crown on her head. Her younger sister stood beside her as Rising Star—a full family moment. Lyla, a junior at Slocomb High School, has been competing in pageants for over a decade. But this title is different. Having survived childhood abuse, she now uses her platform, Fostering Hope, to raise money and support for kids in foster care. She's attended veterans events, participated in parades, and is preparing to compete for Miss Alabama's Teen in March.

stories.title: Local women launch Prom Closet to make Prom affordable for area girls
stories.summary: Two local women launched a Prom Closet to help high school girls attend prom without financial stress. Kalie Hanisch had boutique dresses sitting in storage and wanted to donate them. When she realized no one was running a prom closet this year, she and Traci Sees started one themselves.
stories.extended_summary: Kalie Hanisch had a storage room full of boutique dresses she wanted to donate. She looked around for a local prom closet—a place where high school girls can get formal dresses for free—and found out no one was running one this year. So she and Traci Sees decided to do it themselves. With prom season approaching, they're making sure every girl has the chance to attend without the financial burden of buying a gown. The Prom Closet gives students access to high-quality dresses at no cost.

stories.title: ‘It’s beautiful to see’: Milwaukee youths help transform vacant 53206 lot into a community garden
stories.summary: Milwaukee teens are turning a vacant lot in the 53206 ZIP code into Coaches Corner Garden, set to open in June. The space will grow free produce and flowers for neighbors. We Got This Milwaukee works with youth ages 10-18 to maintain 15 formerly vacant lots, building leadership skills through paid work.
stories.extended_summary: Violence broke out in the neighborhood a few months ago involving a teen. Student leaders Nariah Eubanks, 17, and Denzil Brinson, 16, were hesitant to work on a vacant corner lot because of how unsafe it felt. But they decided to move forward with Coaches Corner Garden anyway—to inspire others and create positive change. "If we're scared, then everyone else is scared too," Eubanks said. The garden, set to open in June, will provide free produce and flowers to residents. We Got This Milwaukee works with youth ages 10-18 to maintain 15 formerly vacant lots in the 53206 ZIP code, building leadership through paid opportunities.

stories.title: Lynnfielders look to put ALS on ice
stories.summary: Lynnfield residents Alex Gentile and Drew Damiani are bringing back "Let's Put ALS on Ice" after last year's event raised over $70,000. The March 28 event at Warrior Ice Arena includes a free family skate with Bruins alumni and a sports memorabilia auction. The movement started to honor Gentile's uncle Jon, a former police officer and hockey player who died from ALS.
stories.extended_summary: Jon Shepard spent most of his life as a police officer and was an avid hockey fan who sometimes filled in as a practice goalie for the Bruins. Then he was diagnosed with ALS. After he died, his brother Jamie started "Let's Put ALS on Ice" to honor him—and last year's event raised over $70,000. Now Alex Gentile, Jon's nephew, and Drew Damiani are bringing it back for a second year. The March 28 event at Warrior Ice Arena includes a free family skate with Bruins alumni and a silent auction. Two teams—the Blue Line Blues and Boston Bruins Alumni—face off on the ice.
